summary:
Chavín de Huántar (c. 900–200 BC) was the principal ceremonial center of the Chavín culture — a monumental stone temple complex at 3,150 meters elevation in the Andean highlands of Peru that served as the focal point of a far-ranging pilgrimage network. The site features an elaborate three-dimensional stone temple with internal galleries, hidden passageways, ventilation systems, and drainage channels that would have been awe-inspiring and disorienting to pilgrims entering the sacred interior.

The main temple (the Castillo or New Temple) contains a complex network of underground galleries lit only by indirect light, in which elaborate carved stone monuments stood — most notably the Lanzón, a 4.5-meter lance-shaped stone with a smiling supernatural deity combining jaguar fangs, serpents, and human features. Sound effects produced by the gallery architecture (including conch-shell trumpets that would create disorienting echoes) enhanced the religious experience. Archaeological evidence of exotic prestige goods — obsidian from distant sources, spondylus shells from Ecuador, tropical birds and plants — documents the long-distance exchange network that both financed and spread Chavín religious influence across the Andes. Chavín de Huántar is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
